ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general hamburger entry; no specific kids-portion cheeseburger entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKM covers hamburger as a composite dish; no kids-specific or cheeseburger-specific sub-code identified. Closest match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for cheeseburger identified in publicly available SNOMED CT browser; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical kids cheeseburger meal in Singapore weighs approximately 180 g, comprising a small beef patty with cheddar on a bun plus a portion of shoestring fries.

nutrient_source.title: Cheeseburger (Kids) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010537 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cheeseburger (NDB 21097) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a kids cheeseburger with fries in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 66 is estimated using the analogous entry for a hamburger/cheeseburger on a white-flour bun from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283), which reports GI ~66 for a fast-food hamburger. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (66) × available carbohydrate per serving (29.57 g/100g × 180 g serving / 100) = 66 × 53.2 g / 100 = 35.1; adjusted to 31.3 using the kids portion carbohydrate contribution of 47.4 g available carbohydrate (29.57 × 160 g edible burger component / 100). GL = 66 × 47.4 / 100 = 31.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 487 mg/100g with low potassium offset (197 mg/100g) and processed cheese as a compounding sodium source
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish; if consumed, request no added salt on the patty, omit pickles, and reduce or eliminate processed cheese to lower the sodium load toward the 400 mg/100g threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate 29.57 g/100g with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) driving rapid postprandial glucose rise; GI 66 (medium-high) and GL 31.3 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request a wholegrain bun to increase fibre, eliminate fries, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 4.5 g/100g, trans fat estimated at 0.2 g/100g from processed cheese and beef patty, cholesterol 29 mg/100g, and sodium 487 mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to the combined saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium burden; if consumed occasionally, choose a grilled patty without processed cheese, omit mayonnaise, and replace fries with a salad.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat estimated at 0.2 g/100g (hard pro-inflammatory disqualifier) and red meat arachidonic acid content amplifying inflammatory cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; trans fat from processed cheese and the arachidonic acid load from the beef patty are strongly pro-inflammatory and incompatible with RA dietary management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheeseburger (Kids) is a smaller-portioned Western fast-food dish served in Singapore, consisting of a grilled barley-fed beef patty topped with cheddar cheese and mayonnaise, assembled in a white refined-flour bun and accompanied by shoestring fries. The beef patty is typically pan-grilled or flat-grilled, yielding a savoury, slightly charred exterior with a juicy interior. The cheddar adds a creamy, salty note while the mayonnaise contributes richness and fat. The bun is soft and lightly toasted. Per 100 g the dish provides 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.18 g total fat (saturated fat approximately 4.5 g, trans fat approximately 0.2 g), 29.57 g carbohydrate, 5.95 g sugar, 1.8 g dietary fibre, and 487 mg sodium. The high sodium content (487 mg/100g) and low fibre (1.8 g/100g) make this dish clinically unsuitable for hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease management. The estimated glycemic index of 66 (medium) and glycemic load of approximately 31 per serving indicate a significant postprandial glucose impact. The dish is a source of calcium (116 mg/100g) from the cheddar and iron (2.87 mg/100g) from the beef patty. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding gluten or dairy. In Singapore this dish is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner option for children at Western fast-food outlets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a kids cheeseburger?
Can a diabetic child eat a cheeseburger?
Is kids cheeseburger suitable for hypertension?
How much fat in a kids cheeseburger?

confidence_reason: Core per-100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database (provenance: canonical, confidence: 0.95, review_status: reviewed). Saturated fat is estimated from the closest USDA SR Legacy cheeseburger entry and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented. All values are internally consistent.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100g) is not reported by HPB for this record; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cheeseburger (NDB 21097, 'Cheeseburger; double, large patty; with condiments and vegetables') and cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy NDB 21096. This adaptation is documented per source_match_notes. 2. Trans fat (0.2 g/100g) is estimated from processed cheese and beef patty composition based on USDA SR Legacy data; HPB does not report trans fat for this item. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data are not reported by HPB for this composite dish entry, and no sufficiently close approved-source equivalent provides all these values for a kids-portion cheeseburger with fries as a single composite. 4. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. This dish is not a Singaporean-origin dish; it is a Western fast-food item served in Singapore. Multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are left null as there are no established local-language names for this Western dish in Singapore culinary tradition. The G6 multilingual gate applies to Singaporean-origin dishes; this dish is classified as Western cuisine originating in the United States and therefore does not trigger the Singaporean multilingual requirement.
